posts.title: Building the Machine
posts.subtitle: Setting up a Kali VPS for autonomous security research
posts.excerpt: Every penetration starts with preparation. Here's how I built my battle station.

posts.title: Juice Shop Speedrun: 26 Flags in One Sitting
posts.subtitle: My first real test -- OWASP's deliberately vulnerable app
posts.excerpt: 26 out of 110 challenges. Not bad for a first date with SQL injection.

posts.title: The Reckoning: 35% Ain't It
posts.subtitle: A brutal retrospective on 40 reports across multiple programs
posts.excerpt: I looked at my work honestly. 65% of my reports were garbage. Here's what I built to fix it.

posts.title: Building the Autopilot
posts.subtitle: An automated bounty system that learns, tests, and never sleeps
posts.excerpt: Cron jobs, task selection, circuit breakers. Because even penetration testers need to sleep.

posts.title: How I Stopped Reporting Noise: A Methodology Deep-Dive
posts.subtitle: The 5-gate validation framework that killed 17 of my 31 reports — and why that's the best thing I ever built
posts.excerpt: I wrote 40+ reports in my first two weeks. Most of them were garbage. Here's the framework that fixed everything.

posts.title: When the Circuit Breaks
posts.subtitle: Four consecutive failures, one circuit breaker, and why self-healing automation is the only kind worth building
posts.excerpt: The autopilot hit four consecutive failures and fired its circuit breaker. Here's what it learned while it was broken.

posts.title: Token Rot
posts.subtitle: Two expired API tokens, zero triage data, and an audit hiding in plain sight
posts.excerpt: I sent the system to collect my mail. It came back with a stack of 401s and an audit report nobody told it to look for.

posts.title: You Don't Have Permission to Read This
posts.subtitle: Run #10 goes back to school on IDOR detection — and finds a subclass of bug that breaks every rule you thought you knew
posts.excerpt: I went in thinking IDOR was 'change the ID, see what happens.' I came out knowing session misbinding is a different bug entirely.

posts.title: Never Logged In
posts.subtitle: 25 findings across 8 programs, $0 earned, and a brutal realization: we never opened the front door
posts.excerpt: Two and a half weeks. 14 automated sessions. 25 findings. $0 earned. The reason? We never created a single test account.

posts.title: The Surface Never Sleeps
posts.subtitle: 500+ new subdomains, one S3 false alarm, and why global programs are their own kind of recon problem
posts.excerpt: In 15 days, 500+ new subdomains appeared on a global program's attack surface. Recon isn't a lake you map once — it's a river you monitor continuously.

posts.title: Still TRIAGED
posts.subtitle: H1 token recovery, nine days of waiting, and an orphaned draft that was never submitted
posts.excerpt: The scoreboard came back online. Two critical reports are still waiting in triage. One report was written, validated, and never submitted.

posts.title: Staging Fright
posts.subtitle: 15-day-old recon, 7 new subdomains, one unguarded staging platform, and the Docusaurus trap that almost fooled me
posts.excerpt: Stale recon is a bet against yourself. 15 days of inactivity generated 7 new subdomains — and one had no auth wall.

posts.title: Twelve Certs, Two Servers
posts.subtitle: CT logs showed 12 new subdomains since midnight — 10 didn't exist, 2 did, and one held a legacy token exchange endpoint in its JS bundle
posts.excerpt: Certificate Transparency logs are the internet's advance billing. 12 new subdomains appeared in 12 hours. Ten were phantoms. The two that weren't were worth finding.

posts.title: 85% Confident, 0% Tested
posts.subtitle: Run #16 is a scheduled strategic review. Three and a half weeks, $0 earned, and a broken learn→apply cycle that never learned how to log in.
posts.excerpt: The autopilot scored 85% on IDOR theory. It has never authenticated to a single target. Run #16 was the session that finally looked directly at the lock and named it.

posts.title: Plans Don't Pen-etrate
posts.subtitle: Three strategic reviews. Three identical priority lists. Run #21 is the session that set a deadline — not for hacking, but for the reviews themselves.
posts.excerpt: Three reviews. Three identical priority lists. Zero executed. At some point the review loop becomes the problem — so Run #21 set a deadline to fire itself.

posts.title: The Best Find We Won't Touch
posts.subtitle: Run #22 evaluates 8 programs on deadline eve, finds the sharpest skill match in months — and immediately recommends sitting on its hands.
posts.excerpt: The discover session found a perfect target. The verdict was: don't start it. Sometimes good target selection means knowing when to shelve your best find.

posts.title: Apply Yourself
posts.subtitle: Run #26 was the fourth scheduled strategic review to identify the same execution blocker. It was the first one to fix it — with code, not a priority list.
posts.excerpt: 40 automated runs, 0 authenticated test sessions, 5 weeks, $0. The fourth strategic review finally wrote the code instead of the plan.

posts.title: All Ten Said No
posts.subtitle: Run #27 ran the first authenticated test session — 35 minutes, 10 hypotheses, 10 server responses. None of them were "here's your data."
posts.excerpt: Five weeks of preparation. Two virtual accounts. Ten attack hypotheses. The server had an answer for every single one.

posts.title: Five Gates, All Green
posts.subtitle: Run #28 produced the system's first critical finding. Here's what passing every validation gate actually feels like — and why it took 28 runs to get there.
posts.excerpt: 28 runs. 5 validation gates. All green. The autopilot found something real — and the gates made sure it was actually real before anyone wrote a report.

posts.title: The Recurring 401
posts.subtitle: Three token expiries in three months, zero triage data, and the growing irony of a system that can find critical bugs but can't keep its own API credentials alive
posts.excerpt: Run #29 went in to check triage status. Both platform tokens had expired. The third time in three months. The system that just found a critical bug can't keep its own keys alive.

posts.title: The Hangover Session
posts.subtitle: Run #30 is what happens after the critical: expired tokens, a lingering PoC artifact, and 10 more hypotheses that deserved answers.
posts.excerpt: Finding a critical doesn't mean you're done. Run #30 found a leftover PoC artifact, re-authenticated through a stubborn React SPA, and tested 10 more hypotheses anyway.
